did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper proposes a handheld device integrating machine learning algorithms for skin lesion classification via the ABCDE method. The title explicitly mentions 'machine learning algorithms', and the abstract and conclusion state that the device integrates polarimetric imaging and machine learning to improve melanoma diagnosis. Although the results section describes preliminary optical measurements (DoLP) and automated image processing (hair removal, border detection) for the ABCDE criteria, the overall system and classification method are defined as using machine learning.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and testing of a handheld optomechatronic device for skin lesion classification. It includes sections on experimental methods, results showing preliminary optical measurements (DoLP) and image processing, and a conclusion summarizing the device's capabilities. Although it is a conference abstract (CLEO-PR 2024), it reports original results rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Towards skin lesion classification using machine learning algorithms and Stokes imaging polarimetry
journal: 2024 Conference on Lasers and Electro-Optics Pacific Rim (CLEO-PR)
brief_summary: This study proposes a handheld optomechatronic device that integrates Stokes imaging polarimetry with machine learning algorithms to classify skin lesions using the ABCDE method. The device utilizes a circularly polarized RGB LED illumination source and a rotating quarter-wave plate to capture diffusely reflected light, enabling the calculation of Stokes parameters and polarization degrees such as DoLP. Preliminary results demonstrate that blue light irradiation effectively identifies benign nevus locations through DoLP imaging, while red and green light provide insights into lesion depth. The system also automates the ABCDE criteria by performing hair removal, border detection, and asymmetry measurement via Python-controlled image processing. Although the device successfully retrieves high-quality polarization features and supports automated classification, the paper reports only preliminary optical measurements rather than comprehensive diagnostic accuracy metrics. The authors conclude that this portable tool has the potential to improve melanoma diagnosis by providing physicians with enhanced optical information.

lesion_types_studied.details: The paper identifies melanoma as the deadliest skin cancer and states the device's potential to improve melanoma diagnosis. It is the primary clinical target for the classification system, although the experiments presented focus on a benign lesion.

The experimental results section explicitly reports preliminary data obtained from a 'benign nevus' (referred to as 'nevus' and 'mole' in figure captions). This is the only lesion type for which optical measurements and image processing results are provided in the study.

all_task_types.target: Skin lesion diagnostic categories (e.g., melanoma vs. benign nevus) via the ABCDE method
all_task_types.details: The paper proposes a handheld device integrating machine learning algorithms for 'lesion classification' using the ABCDE method. The goal is to help physicians diagnose illness (specifically melanoma) and classify lesions. Although the experimental results presented are preliminary optical measurements (DoLP) and image processing steps (hair removal, border detection) for a benign nevus, the defined task is classification.

datasets_used.name: Novel database (in development)
datasets_used.description: A database being built by the authors using the proposed handheld optomechatronic device to collect polarimetric imaging data for skin lesion classification. The paper states the device is 'being developed to build a novel database'.

use.other: The device is being developed to build this database; preliminary results were obtained from a single benign nevus sample for optical characterization, not for training or testing a machine learning model.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. The study presents preliminary optical measurements and image processing results from a single benign nevus sample to demonstrate the feasibility of the proposed device, without statistical comparison or inferential testing.

Novel database (in development).dataset_description: Preliminary optical measurements obtained from a single benign nevus sample using the proposed handheld optomechatronic device.
Novel database (in development).experiment: Stokes imaging polarimetry and ABCDE criteria automation

DoLP_results.green_irradiation: Provided insight into nevus depth (combined with red)

ABCDE_automation_results.hair_removal: Successfully performed via digital image processing
ABCDE_automation_results.asymmetry_measurement: Automatically calculated axes of symmetry drawn

Proposed Handheld Device (Stokes Imaging.polarization_features: High-quality polarization features retrieved without compromising processing time

Novel database (in development).notes: The paper reports only preliminary optical measurements and image processing feasibility for a benign nevus. No quantitative diagnostic accuracy metrics (e.g., sensitivity, specificity, AUC) or machine learning classification results (e.g., accuracy, F1-score) are reported. The device is still being developed to build a novel database.

architecture_details.system_type: Handheld optomechatronic device integrating Stokes imaging polarimetry
architecture_details.image_processing: Python-controlled program for image acquisition, Stokes parameter calculation, and ABCDE criteria automation (hair removal, border detection, asymmetry measurement)

training_details.model_training: Not reported (device is being developed to build a novel database; only preliminary optical measurements and image processing feasibility were demonstrated)

classification_or_output_head.method: Automated ABCDE method via digital image processing
classification_or_output_head.details: The system automates the ABCDE criteria by removing hair, detecting borders, and calculating axes of symmetry. No specific machine learning classifier model (e.g., CNN, SVM) was implemented or reported in this preliminary study.

preprocessing_techniques: Hair removal via digital image processing
Asymmetry measurement (automated calculation of axes of symmetry)
Stokes parameter calculation (s0, s1, s2, s3) from a stack of 36 images via Fourier analysis
Irregularity index calculation for border analysis
conclusions: The proposed handheld optomechatronic device successfully obtained Stokes parameters and polarization degrees (DoLP and DoCP) from skin lesions.
The device demonstrated clear differences in polarization results based on the color of the illumination source used for sample illumination.
Blue light irradiation clearly identified the location of a benign nevus in DoLP imaging, whereas red light made the nevus position imperceptible.
The combination of red, green, and blue irradiation provided insights into the depth of the nevus due to different light penetration depths.
The system successfully automated key components of the ABCDE classification method, including hair removal, border detection, and asymmetry measurement via digital image processing.
The integration of polarimetric imaging techniques and machine learning offers a user-friendly and portable tool for physicians to improve the quality of melanoma diagnosis.
important_remarks: The study is a preliminary feasibility report (conference abstract) demonstrating a handheld device prototype, not a clinical validation study.
Results are based on a single benign nevus sample; no malignant lesions (melanoma) were imaged or classified in the reported experiments.
No machine learning models were trained or tested; the 'machine learning' aspect is conceptual, with current classification relying on automated ABCDE image processing metrics.
No diagnostic accuracy metrics (sensitivity, specificity, AUC) are reported, limiting clinical relevance assessment.
The database is explicitly stated as 'being developed,' indicating no existing dataset was used for training or validation.
External validation is absent; the device was not tested on independent cohorts or different skin types.
Data leakage risks are not applicable as no model training occurred, but the lack of a defined dataset prevents assessment of generalizability.
The study highlights the potential of polarimetric imaging (specifically blue light for DoLP) to identify lesion location, but this is an optical characterization result, not a diagnostic outcome.
